title: Wilhelm von Rümann

(11 November 1850 in Hanover – 6 February 1906 in [Ajaccio](/wiki/Ajaccio "Ajaccio")) was a prominent German sculptor, based in [Munich](/wiki/Munich "Munich").

German sculptor

| Wilhelm von Rümann | |
| --- | --- |

**Wilhelm von Rümann** (11 November 1850 in Hanover – 6 February 1906 in [Ajaccio](/wiki/Ajaccio "Ajaccio")) was a prominent German sculptor, based in [Munich](/wiki/Munich "Munich").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/52/Nuernberg-Reiterstandbild_Wilhelm_I.jpg/250px-Nuernberg-Reiterstandbild_Wilhelm_I.jpg)](/wiki/File:Nuernberg-Reiterstandbild_Wilhelm_I.jpg)

Equestrian monument of [Kaiser Wilhelm I](/wiki/Kaiser_Wilhelm_I "Kaiser Wilhelm I") in [Nuremberg](/wiki/Nuremberg "Nuremberg"), begun by [Syrius Eberle](/wiki/Syrius_Eberle "Syrius Eberle")

## Life

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Wilhelm_von_R%C3%BCmann&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Life")]

Rümann was born in [Hanover](/wiki/Hanover "Hanover"). He studied from 1872 to 1874 at the [Academy of Fine Arts, Munich](/wiki/Academy_of_Fine_Arts,_Munich "Academy of Fine Arts, Munich") (*Akademie der Bildenden Künste München*), and from 1880 with [Michael Wagmüller](/wiki/Michael_Wagm%C3%BCller "Michael Wagmüller").[[1]](#cite_note-1) From 1887 he taught at the Academy of Fine Arts, Munich. In 1891 he was raised to the nobility.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1d/N%C3%BCrnberg_Art_Nouveau_Silver_Medal_1906_by_Ruemann%2C_reverse.jpg/250px-N%C3%BCrnberg_Art_Nouveau_Silver_Medal_1906_by_Ruemann%2C_reverse.jpg)](/wiki/File:N%C3%BCrnberg_Art_Nouveau_Silver_Medal_1906_by_Ruemann,_reverse.jpg)

Nürnberg, [Art Nouveau](/wiki/Art_Nouveau "Art Nouveau") Silver Medal 1905 by Ruemann, reverse. This large medal was given by the city of Nürnberg to the Royalties attending the ceremony of the unveiling of the monument in honour of Kaiser Wilhelm I on 14 December 1905 in Nuremberg.

As well as numerous funerary monuments in the [Alter Südfriedhof](/wiki/Alter_S%C3%BCdfriedhof "Alter Südfriedhof") (Old South Burial Ground) in Munich, he created sculptures which are still to be seen in the city: monuments for [Georg Simon Ohm](/wiki/Georg_Simon_Ohm "Georg Simon Ohm") (1895, in the courtyard of the [Technische Universität München](/wiki/Technische_Universit%C3%A4t_M%C3%BCnchen "Technische Universität München")), [Max von Pettenkofer](/wiki/Max_von_Pettenkofer "Max von Pettenkofer") (1909) and [Carl von Effner](/wiki/Carl_von_Effner "Carl von Effner") (1886) at the Maximiliansplatz (now the [Lenbachplatz](/wiki/Lenbachplatz "Lenbachplatz")), the *Puttenbrunnen* (Putti Fountain) at the Peace Monument in the [Prinzregentenstraße](/wiki/Prinzregentenstra%C3%9Fe_(Munich) "Prinzregentenstraße (Munich)") (originally intended for [Schloss Herrenchiemsee](/wiki/Schloss_Herrenchiemsee "Schloss Herrenchiemsee")) and the marble lions in front of the [Feldherrnhalle](/wiki/Feldherrnhalle "Feldherrnhalle") (1906).

Among his pupils were [Bernhard Bleeker](/wiki/Bernhard_Bleeker "Bernhard Bleeker"), [Emil Julius Epple](/wiki/Emil_Julius_Epple "Emil Julius Epple"), [Jakob Hofmann](/w/index.php?title=Jakob_Hofmann&action=edit&redlink=1 "Jakob Hofmann (page does not exist)"), [Moissey Kogan](/wiki/Moissey_Kogan "Moissey Kogan"), [Martin Scheible](/w/index.php?title=Martin_Scheible&action=edit&redlink=1 "Martin Scheible (page does not exist)") and [Alois Mayer](/wiki/Alois_Mayer "Alois Mayer").

He died in [Ajaccio](/wiki/Ajaccio "Ajaccio"), Corsica, and is buried in the [Nordfriedhof](/wiki/Nordfriedhof_(Munich) "Nordfriedhof (Munich)") ("Northern Cemetery"), Munich.

## Works (public monuments)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Wilhelm_von_R%C3%BCmann&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Works (public monuments)")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/9d/Woerth_monument_bavarois.jpg/250px-Woerth_monument_bavarois.jpg)](/wiki/File:Woerth_monument_bavarois.jpg)

Bavarian War Memorial, Woerth

* [Chemnitz](/wiki/Chemnitz "Chemnitz"):
  + [Kaiser Wilhelm I](/wiki/Kaiser_Wilhelm_I "Kaiser Wilhelm I"): equestrian statue in the market place; sent for scrap after 1945
  + [Bismarck](/wiki/Otto_von_Bismarck "Otto von Bismarck") Statue (1899), to the left; sent for scrap after 1945
  + [Moltke](/wiki/Helmuth_von_Moltke_the_Elder "Helmuth von Moltke the Elder") Statue, to the right; sent for scrap after 1945
* [Heilbronn](/wiki/Heilbronn "Heilbronn"):
  + Kaiser Wilhelm I Monument (1895), originally in front of the Harmonie Concert and Congress Centre, now in the *Alter Friedhof* (Old Graveyard) in the Weinsberger Straße
  + [Robert Mayer](/wiki/Julius_von_Mayer "Julius von Mayer") Statue, in the market place
* Munich:
  + Monument of [Georg Simon Ohm](/wiki/Georg_Simon_Ohm "Georg Simon Ohm"), 1895
  + Funerary monument of [Princess Ludovika of Bavaria](/wiki/Princess_Ludovika_of_Bavaria "Princess Ludovika of Bavaria")
  + Several statues of [Prince Luitpold of Bavaria, the Prince Regent](/wiki/Luitpold,_Prince_Regent_of_Bavaria "Luitpold, Prince Regent of Bavaria"), including one in the south-west of Siedlung Neuhausen
  + Bust of [Princess Theresa of Bavaria](/wiki/Princess_Theresa_of_Bavaria "Princess Theresa of Bavaria") (in the [Bavarian Academy of Sciences and Humanities](/wiki/Bavarian_Academy_of_Sciences_and_Humanities "Bavarian Academy of Sciences and Humanities"))
  + Bust of [Wilhelm Bauer](/wiki/Wilhelm_Bauer "Wilhelm Bauer") in the [Deutsches Museum](/wiki/Deutsches_Museum "Deutsches Museum")
  + and many others
* [Nuremberg](/wiki/Nuremberg "Nuremberg"):
  + Kaiser Wilhelm I equestrian statue (1905) (designed and begun by [Syrius Eberle](/wiki/Syrius_Eberle "Syrius Eberle"), who died in 1903; completed by Rümann), Egidienplatz, in front of the Pellerhaus
  + Statue of Prince Regent Luitpold (1901), station forecourt, removed in 1934, melted down in 1939
* [Stuttgart](/wiki/Stuttgart "Stuttgart"):
  + Kaiser Wilhelm I equestrian statue (1898)
* [Bad Urach](/wiki/Bad_Urach "Bad Urach"): bust of Bismarck in the Schloßstraße
* [Woerth](/wiki/Woerth "Woerth"), Alsace, France: Bavarian National War Memorial 1870, made 1889
